# The AI Generalist Stack: 4 Layers That Make Solopreneurs Unreasonably Effective

Most advice for AI solopreneurs is a tool dump. Someone lists 30 apps, tells you to subscribe, and wishes you luck. That is not a stack. A stack has layers, and each layer does one job that the layer above it cannot do for itself.

After running a one-person AI consultancy for 18 months and shipping 12 client projects, I have a stack that I actually defend. It is 4 layers. Nothing more, nothing fewer.

## Layer 1: Reasoning — your brain in a browser tab

At the bottom is a reasoning model you trust enough to argue with. For me that is Claude Opus for long-context planning and Claude Sonnet for execution. The point is not the brand. The point is that when you get stuck, you should be able to paste your entire problem — code, context, constraints — into one place and have it push back.

If you are still using the free tier of anything, you are skipping this layer. A $200/month subscription to one serious reasoning model will earn back 40 hours in the first week. I am not speaking abstractly. I logged it.

## Layer 2: Execution — agents that do, not chat

The second layer is where most solopreneurs get stuck. They use the reasoning model to plan, then open 12 browser tabs to execute. That is not leverage. That is just a more expensive version of doing it yourself.

An execution layer means agents that actually touch systems. Claude Code editing your repo. An n8n workflow posting your newsletter. A Retool dashboard generating client invoices from a spreadsheet. The test is this: after you close the laptop, is work still happening? If yes, you have an execution layer. If no, you have an expensive chatbot.

## Layer 3: Memory — context that outlives sessions

Memory is the layer nobody talks about until it breaks. Every time you start a new Claude session and have to re-explain who your client is, what your product does, and what you tried yesterday — that is a memory failure.

My memory layer is three things stacked: a CLAUDE.md file per project (rules, patterns, gotchas), a Notion database of client context (past decisions, preferences, brand voice), and a personal knowledge graph I query with NotebookLM. The goal is that my agents know what I know, without me re-explaining it.

## Layer 4: Distribution — the output finds its audience

The top layer is where most AI content stops short. You can build a brilliant thing and nobody will see it. Distribution is not a single channel — it is the pattern of converting every output from layers 1-3 into something a human reads, watches, or clicks.

For me that pattern is: blog post on my site, LinkedIn carousel from the blog, short video clip from the carousel, newsletter from all three. Four artifacts from one idea, each living on a different platform, each feeding Knowledge Graph signals back to my domain.

This is the unsexy layer. Nobody tweets about their distribution pipeline. But without it, layers 1-3 are a very expensive hobby.

When I onboard another solopreneur, I do not ask which tools they use. I ask which layer breaks first when they try to scale. Nine times out of ten, it is memory — they cannot keep context across sessions, so every client interaction starts from zero. The stack is the answer. Fix the layer, not the tool.
